1945
The world’s table

The United

Nations

A short history and field guide to the world’s largest organisation, and to its First Committee on disarmament and security, DISEC.

I
Chapter One

Why the world

built the UN

Every generation before ours tried, and failed, to end war. The UN is the latest and most serious attempt.

The oldest problem

“For thousands of years, powerful states settled their differences the same way: with armies. The strong took what they could, and the weak suffered what they must.”

The twentieth century made that habit unbearable. Industrial weapons could now kill on a scale no one had imagined. After two world wars in thirty years, the survivors decided the old way had to end.

1920
The first attempt

The League

of Nations

After the First World War killed around 20 million people, the victors built the first global body for peace. Its big idea was collective security: an attack on one member would count as an attack on all.

01

Founded in 1920

Based in Geneva, inspired by US President Woodrow Wilson and his Fourteen Points.

02

Up to 58 members

Most of the world joined at some point during its life.

03

Talk, not fight

Disputes were meant to be settled by discussion and, if needed, shared pressure.

1939

Strong on paper, weak in practice

•

The United States never joined

Its Senate refused to ratify the treaty, so the League lost the very power that had proposed it.

•

It had no army

The League could ask states to act, but it could never make them. Enforcement depended on goodwill.

•

Every decision needed a unanimous vote

One refusal could freeze the whole body, so bold action was almost impossible.

•

It could not stop aggression

Japan invaded Manchuria in 1931. Italy invaded Ethiopia in 1935. Germany rearmed and expanded. The League watched, powerless.

By 1939 the world was at war again. The experiment had failed.

The reckoning

The Second World War

From 1939 to 1945, the deadliest conflict in human history forced the world to think again.

70 to 85m

people killed, soldiers and civilians alike, across the world.

6m

Jews murdered in the Holocaust, along with millions of others.

2

atomic bombs ended the war and opened a frightening new age.

Out of the horror came one promise, repeated everywhere: never again.

1945
A new beginning

The UN is born

01

The name “United Nations” was coined in 1942 by US President Franklin Roosevelt.

02

In 1945, fifty countries met at the San Francisco Conference to write the UN Charter.

03

The Charter was signed on 26 June and took effect on 24 October 1945, now UN Day.

51 founding members. Today, 193.

II
Chapter Two

What the UN

is for

Its founding Charter opens with a set of purposes and principles that still guide every debate today.

Four purposes

01

Keep the peace

Maintain international peace and security, and stop threats before they grow.

02

Build friendship

Develop friendly relations between nations based on equal rights and self-determination.

03

Solve problems together

Cooperate on economic, social and humanitarian problems, and defend human rights for all.

04

Be a common centre

Serve as the place where nations bring their actions into line with one another.

Four principles

01

Sovereign equality

Every state is legally equal, whether it is vast or tiny, rich or poor.

02

Peaceful settlement

Disputes must be solved by talking, mediation or courts, not by war.

03

No use of force

States must not threaten or attack the territory or independence of others.

04

Non-interference

The UN does not meddle in matters that are essentially inside a country. This principle is tested constantly, as our case studies will show.

III
Chapter Three

How the UN

is built

Six principal organs, each with a different job. Two of them matter most for us.

Six Main Organs

01

General Assembly

The whole membership. Every state, one vote.

02

Security Council

Keeps the peace, and alone may authorise force.

03

Secretariat

The staff who run the UN, led by the Secretary-General.

04

Court of Justice

Fifteen judges in The Hague who settle disputes between states.

05

Economic and Social Council

Coordinates development, health and human welfare.

06

Trusteeship Council

Guided colonies to independence. Suspended in 1994.

01 · The General Assembly
0
•

member states, each with one equal vote.

•

Important questions need a two-thirds majority to pass.

•

Its resolutions carry great moral weight, but are not legally binding.

02 · The Security Council

The one that

can compel

15

members in total

Ten are elected for two-year terms.

5

permanent members

The United States, United Kingdom, France, Russia and China.

✓

binding power

It can impose sanctions, deploy peacekeepers, and authorise the use of force.

The problem of power

The veto

Each of the five permanent members can block any decision with a single “no” vote.

They were given this power in 1945 so the great powers would stay inside the UN, not walk away as they had from the League.

The cost: when a permanent member or its ally is involved, the Council can freeze completely.

The rest of the machine

•

The Secretariat

Tens of thousands of staff who run the UN day to day, led by the Secretary-General.

•

The International Court of Justice

The UN’s main court, in The Hague, settling legal disputes between states. You will meet it again in the Sudan case.

•

The Economic and Social Council

Fifty-four members coordinating the UN’s vast work on development and human welfare.

The chief diplomat

António

Guterres

Secretary-General since 2017

•

The chief administrator of the UN, and its main voice to the world.

•

He can raise any matter that threatens peace before the Security Council.

•

His second term ends in December 2026, so a successor may be chosen this year.

IV
Chapter Four

How the UN

acts

From a quiet word between diplomats to soldiers on the ground, the UN has a ladder of tools, and clear limits.

A Ladder of Tools

01
Diplomacy

Quiet mediation between rivals.

02
Resolutions

A vote sets an official position.

03
Peacekeeping

Blue helmets, first sent in 1948.

04
Sanctions

Economic pressure instead of war.

05
Agencies

WHO, UNHCR, UNICEF and the WFP.

Four hard limits

•

Sovereignty

It cannot simply override what a country does inside its own borders.

•

The veto

One permanent member can freeze the Security Council on the gravest issues.

•

No army of its own

Every peacekeeper is a soldier lent, and taken back, by a member state.

•

It depends on members

The UN works only as far as its states are willing to pay and cooperate.

Chapter Five

Meet DISEC

The First Committee of the General Assembly

First Committee · since 1945

DISEC

Disarmament and International Security Committee

The First Committee of the General Assembly, created when the UN began.

Every one of the 193 members holds a seat and one vote.

Its field: weapons, war, and the threats that cross borders.

What DISEC

deals with

•

Conventional weapons

From rifles to tanks. The AK47 is the most widespread weapon on Earth.

•

Weapons of mass destruction

Nuclear, chemical and biological arms.

•

New dangers

Cyber attacks, armed drones, autonomous weapons and the militarisation of space.

Landmark agreements

DISEC is where states debate, build and defend arms agreements like these.

1968

Non-Proliferation Treaty

Slows the spread of nuclear weapons and works toward disarmament.

1972

Biological Weapons Convention

Bans the making and stockpiling of biological arms.

1993

Chemical Weapons Convention

Bans chemical weapons and requires existing stocks to be destroyed.

2013

Arms Trade Treaty

Sets rules for the global trade in conventional weapons.

Strong voice, no army

What DISEC does

Recommend

Writes and passes resolutions

Builds shared global rules and norms

Puts political pressure on states

What it cannot do

Compel

Cannot send troops or use force

Cannot punish a state on its own

Only the Security Council can compel

Five facts to lock in

01

The League of Nations failed partly because the United States never joined.

02

The UN Charter took effect on 24 October 1945.

03

The UN has 193 members and six main organs.

04

The Security Council has 15 members, and 5 hold a veto.

05

DISEC is the First Committee: disarmament and international security.

The takeaway

Words before weapons

That is the whole idea of the UN. DISEC is where the hardest of those conversations happen.
